info.name: Laravel API
info.description:
# Laravel API ŌĆö Complete Reference

## Overview

The **Laravel API** is a RESTful web service built on the [Laravel PHP framework](https://laravel.com/), one of the most popular and expressive PHP frameworks in the world. Laravel provides an elegant syntax, a powerful ORM (Eloquent), built-in validation, routing, and a rich ecosystem of tools that make building APIs fast, reliable, and maintainable.

This API exposes structured application data over HTTP in JSON format. It follows REST (Representational State Transfer) architectural principles:

- Each **URL** identifies a unique resource or collection of resources (e.g., `/api/categories` represents all categories).
- **HTTP methods** define the action: `GET` retrieves data, `POST` creates a new resource, `PATCH` updates an existing resource, `DELETE` removes a resource.
- **Responses are stateless**: every request is self-contained and includes all the information the server needs to process it. No session state is maintained between requests.
- **Responses are self-describing**: the HTTP status code, response headers, and JSON body together fully communicate the outcome of every request.

This collection documents every available endpoint in the Laravel API, including required headers, full request body schemas, complete response structures with field-by-field explanations, HTTP status code semantics, and practical usage guidelines.

The API is currently configured for **local development** and runs on your machine at `http://127.0.0.1:8000`. All API endpoints are prefixed with `/api/`.

---

## Base URL

```
http://127.0.0.1:8000
```

All endpoint paths in this collection are relative to this base URL. To avoid hardcoding this value, create a Postman environment variable named `base_url` with the value `http://127.0.0.1:8000` and reference it as `{{base_url}}/api/...` in all requests.

---

## Endpoints Summary

| # | Name | Method | Path | Description |
|---|------|--------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Get Categories | `GET` | `/api/categories` | Retrieve all categories |
| 2 | Post Categories | `POST` | `/api/categories` | Create a new category |
| 3 | Single Category | `GET` | `/api/categories/{id}` | Retrieve one category by ID |
| 4 | Update Category | `PATCH` | `/api/categories/{id}` | Partially update a category by ID |
| 5 | Delete Category | `DELETE` | `/api/categories/{id}` | Delete a category by ID |

---

## Required Request Headers

Every request to this API must include the correct HTTP headers.

### `Accept: application/json`

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Header name** | `Accept` |
| **Header value** | `application/json` |
| **Required for** | All requests (GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE) |

The `Accept` header tells the server what media type the client can understand. When set to `application/json`, Laravel's exception handler returns all responses ŌĆö including validation errors and server errors ŌĆö as JSON objects rather than HTML pages.

Without this header:
- A `404 Not Found` returns a full Laravel HTML error page.
- A `422 Unprocessable Entity` returns an HTML page instead of a structured JSON error.
- A `500 Internal Server Error` returns an HTML debug page.

### `Content-Type: application/json`

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Header name** | `Content-Type` |
| **Header value** | `application/json` |
| **Required for** | `POST` and `PATCH` requests that send a JSON body |

Tells the server the format of the data in the request body. Without this header on a POST or PATCH request, Laravel will not parse the JSON body and `$request->input('name')` will return `null`, causing a `422` validation error.

> **Postman tip:** When you select **Body ŌåÆ raw ŌåÆ JSON**, Postman adds `Content-Type: application/json` automatically.

---

## Category Resource Schema

A **category** is the primary resource exposed by this API. Every endpoint that returns category data uses this schema.

| Field | Type | Nullable | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
| `id` | `integer` | No | Auto-incremented primary key. Assigned by the database. Immutable after creation. |
| `name` | `string` | No | Human-readable display name. UTF-8 encoded. Required at creation. |
| `description` | `string` | Yes | Optional longer description. `null` if not provided. |
| `created_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | UTC timestamp of creation. Format: `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS.000000Z`. Set by Laravel automatically. |
| `updated_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | UTC timestamp of last update. Equals `created_at` if never updated. Set by Laravel automatically. |

**Example category object:**
```json
{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "Electronics",
  "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories",
  "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-03-22T14:45:00.000000Z"
}
```

---

## Endpoint Reference

---

### 1. Get Categories

**Retrieve a list of all categories stored in the database.**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Method** | `GET` |
| **Path** | `/api/categories` |
| **Full URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories` |
| **Auth required** | No |
| **Request body** | None |

#### Purpose

This endpoint queries the `categories` database table and returns every record as a JSON array. It is the primary read endpoint for listing all available categories. Use it to populate dropdowns, navigation menus, filter panels, or any UI component that needs the full list of categories.

The response is always an array ŌĆö even if there are zero records, the server returns an empty array `[]` with a `200 OK` status. This is intentional: an empty collection is not an error.

#### Required Headers

| Header | Value | Required |
|--------|-------|----------|
| `Accept` | `application/json` | Yes |

#### URL Parameters

None. This endpoint does not accept any URL path parameters.

#### Query Parameters

None defined by default. The base Laravel implementation does not include pagination, filtering, or sorting parameters unless explicitly added to the controller.

#### Request Body

None. `GET` requests do not have a request body.

#### Success Response ŌĆö `200 OK`

Returned when the server successfully queries the database, regardless of how many records exist.

**Response body:** A JSON array of category objects. Returns `[]` if no categories exist.

```json
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "Electronics",
    "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories",
    "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z"
  },
  {
    "id": 2,
    "name": "Books",
    "description": null,
    "created_at": "2024-01-16T11:00:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-16T11:00:00.000000Z"
  },
  {
    "id": 3,
    "name": "Clothing",
    "description": "Apparel and fashion items",
    "created_at": "2024-02-01T08:00:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-02-01T08:00:00.000000Z"
  }
]
```

**Empty database response (still `200 OK`):**
```json
[]
```

#### Error Responses

| Code | Condition | Response Body |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Database is unreachable, migration not run, or unhandled PHP exception | `{"message": "Server Error"}` |

#### Notes

- The order of records in the array is determined by the database's default ordering (typically insertion order / ascending `id`).
- There is no pagination by default. If the database grows large, consider adding `paginate()` in the controller.
- The `description` field may be `null` for any record ŌĆö always handle this in your client code.

---

### 2. Post Categories

**Create a new category resource in the database.**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Method** | `POST` |
| **Path** | `/api/categories` |
| **Full URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories` |
| **Auth required** | No |
| **Request body** | JSON object |

#### Purpose

This endpoint accepts a JSON body containing the data for a new category, validates it against Laravel's validation rules, inserts a new record into the `categories` table, and returns the newly created category object ŌĆö including the auto-assigned `id` and timestamps generated by the database.

Use this endpoint whenever a user creates a new category through your application's UI (e.g., an admin panel form, a category creation modal, or a CLI tool).

#### Required Headers

| Header | Value | Required |
|--------|-------|----------|
| `Accept` | `application/json` | Yes |
| `Content-Type` | `application/json` | Yes |

#### URL Parameters

None.

#### Request Body Fields

The request body must be a valid JSON object. The following fields are accepted:

| Field | Type | Required | Validation Rules | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-----------------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | **Yes** | Required, must be a non-empty string | The display name of the new category. |
| `description` | `string` | No | Optional, must be a string if provided | A longer description of the category. Omit or set to `null` to leave blank. |

**Minimal valid request body (name only):**
```json
{
  "name": "Sports"
}
```

**Full request body (name and description):**
```json
{
  "name": "Sports",
  "description": "Outdoor and fitness equipment"
}
```

#### Success Response ŌĆö `201 Created`

Returned when the category is successfully validated and inserted into the database.

**Response body:** The complete newly created category object, including the auto-assigned `id`, `created_at`, and `updated_at` timestamps.

```json
{
  "id": 5,
  "name": "Sports",
  "description": "Outdoor and fitness equipment",
  "created_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z"
}
```

> **Important:** Save the returned `id` value. You will need it to reference this category in future `GET /api/categories/{id}`, `PATCH /api/categories/{id}`, and `DELETE /api/categories/{id}` requests.

#### Error Responses

| Code | Condition | Response Body |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| `422 Unprocessable Entity` | `name` field is missing, empty, or fails validation | See below |
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Database unreachable or unhandled exception | `{"message": "Server Error"}` |

**`422` response body ŌĆö missing `name`:**
```json
{
  "message": "The name field is required.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "The name field is required."
    ]
  }
}
```

**`422` response body ŌĆö `name` is not a string:**
```json
{
  "message": "The name field must be a string.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "The name field must be a string."
    ]
  }
}
```

The `errors` object maps each failing field name to an array of human-readable error messages. Iterate over this object in your client to display inline validation feedback.

#### Notes

- `created_at` and `updated_at` are set automatically by Laravel's Eloquent ORM. Do not include them in the request body.
- `id` is assigned by the database auto-increment. Do not include it in the request body.
- If you omit `description`, the field will be stored as `null` and returned as `null` in all future responses.

---

### 3. Single Category

**Retrieve a single category by its unique numeric ID.**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Method** | `GET` |
| **Path** | `/api/categories/{id}` |
| **Full URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories/1` |
| **Auth required** | No |
| **Request body** | None |

#### Purpose

This endpoint looks up a single category record in the database by its primary key (`id`) and returns it as a JSON object. Use it when you need the full details of one specific category ŌĆö for example, to populate a category detail page, pre-fill an edit form, or verify that a category was created correctly after a `POST` request.

The `{id}` in the URL path is a **route parameter** ŌĆö replace it with the actual integer ID of the category you want to retrieve (e.g., `/api/categories/3` to fetch the category with `id = 3`).

#### Required Headers

| Header | Value | Required |
|--------|-------|----------|
| `Accept` | `application/json` | Yes |

#### URL Path Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
| `id` | `integer` | **Yes** | The unique numeric ID of the category to retrieve. Must be a positive integer that exists in the database. |

**Example:** To retrieve the category with ID 7:
```
GET http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories/7
```

#### Query Parameters

None.

#### Request Body

None. `GET` requests do not have a request body.

#### Success Response ŌĆö `200 OK`

Returned when a category with the given `id` exists in the database.

**Response body:** A single category JSON object.

```json
{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "Electronics",
  "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories",
  "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z"
}
```

#### Error Responses

| Code | Condition | Response Body |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| `404 Not Found` | No category with the given `id` exists in the database | `{"message": "Not Found"}` |
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Database unreachable or unhandled exception | `{"message": "Server Error"}` |

**`404` response body:**
```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

This response is returned when Laravel's `findOrFail()` (or `Route Model Binding`) cannot locate a record with the specified ID. It does **not** mean the URL is wrong ŌĆö it means the record does not exist.

#### Notes

- The `{id}` must be a positive integer. Passing a non-integer (e.g., `/api/categories/abc`) will result in a `404` because Laravel's router will not match the route.
- Use the `id` values returned by `GET /api/categories` or `POST /api/categories` to build valid URLs for this endpoint.
- This endpoint is idempotent: calling it multiple times with the same ID always returns the same result (assuming the record has not been modified).

---

### 4. Update Category

**Partially update an existing category by its unique numeric ID.**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Method** | `PATCH` |
| **Path** | `/api/categories/{id}` |
| **Full URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories/1` |
| **Auth required** | No |
| **Request body** | JSON object (partial update) |

#### Purpose

This endpoint updates one or more fields of an existing category record. `PATCH` is a **partial update** method ŌĆö you only need to include the fields you want to change. Fields you omit are left unchanged in the database.

Use this endpoint when a user edits a category in your application's admin panel, renames a category, or updates its description without changing its name.

**`PATCH` vs `PUT`:** `PUT` requires you to send the complete resource representation (all fields), replacing the entire record. `PATCH` only requires the fields you want to modify. This API uses `PATCH` for partial updates, which is the recommended approach for most real-world update operations.

#### Required Headers

| Header | Value | Required |
|--------|-------|----------|
| `Accept` | `application/json` | Yes |
| `Content-Type` | `application/json` | Yes |

#### URL Path Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
| `id` | `integer` | **Yes** | The unique numeric ID of the category to update. Must exist in the database. |

**Example:** To update the category with ID 3:
```
PATCH http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories/3
```

#### Request Body Fields

Send only the fields you want to update. All fields are optional for `PATCH`.

| Field | Type | Required | Validation Rules | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-----------------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | No | Must be a non-empty string if provided | The new display name for the category. |
| `description` | `string` or `null` | No | Must be a string or null if provided | The new description. Pass `null` to clear it. |

**Update only the name:**
```json
{
  "name": "Consumer Electronics"
}
```

**Update only the description:**
```json
{
  "description": "All electronic gadgets and devices"
}
```

**Update both fields:**
```json
{
  "name": "Consumer Electronics",
  "description": "All electronic gadgets and devices"
}
```

**Clear the description (set to null):**
```json
{
  "description": null
}
```

#### Success Response ŌĆö `200 OK`

Returned when the category is found, the request body passes validation, and the record is successfully updated.

**Response body:** The complete updated category object, reflecting all changes.

```json
{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "Consumer Electronics",
  "description": "All electronic gadgets and devices",
  "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-06-18T10:00:00.000000Z"
}
```

Note that `updated_at` is automatically set to the current UTC timestamp by Laravel's Eloquent ORM when the record is saved. `created_at` remains unchanged.

#### Error Responses

| Code | Condition | Response Body |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| `404 Not Found` | No category with the given `id` exists | `{"message": "Not Found"}` |
| `422 Unprocessable Entity` | A provided field fails validation (e.g., `name` is an empty string) | See below |
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Database unreachable or unhandled exception | `{"message": "Server Error"}` |

**`404` response body:**
```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

**`422` response body ŌĆö `name` sent as empty string:**
```json
{
  "message": "The name field must be a string.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "The name field must be a string."
    ]
  }
}
```

#### Notes

- Sending an empty JSON body `{}` is valid for `PATCH` ŌĆö it results in no changes and returns the current state of the category with `200 OK`.
- `id`, `created_at`, and `updated_at` cannot be changed via this endpoint. Even if you include them in the request body, they will be ignored.
- Always retrieve the current state of the category first (`GET /api/categories/{id}`) before updating, to avoid overwriting data unintentionally.

---

### 5. Delete Category

**Permanently delete a category by its unique numeric ID.**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Method** | `DELETE` |
| **Path** | `/api/categories/{id}` |
| **Full URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories/1` |
| **Auth required** | No |
| **Request body** | None |

#### Purpose

This endpoint permanently removes a category record from the `categories` database table. The deletion is **irreversible** ŌĆö once a category is deleted, it cannot be recovered unless you have a database backup or implement soft deletes in Laravel.

Use this endpoint when a user removes a category from your application's admin panel, or when you need to clean up test data during development.

**Warning:** If other database tables reference the deleted category's `id` as a foreign key (e.g., a `products` table with a `category_id` column), deleting the category may cause a database constraint violation (`500` error) or leave orphaned records, depending on your foreign key configuration. Always verify referential integrity before deleting.

#### Required Headers

| Header | Value | Required |
|--------|-------|----------|
| `Accept` | `application/json` | Yes |

#### URL Path Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
| `id` | `integer` | **Yes** | The unique numeric ID of the category to delete. Must exist in the database. |

**Example:** To delete the category with ID 5:
```
DELETE http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories/5
```

#### Query Parameters

None.

#### Request Body

None. `DELETE` requests do not require a request body.

#### Success Response ŌĆö `200 OK` or `204 No Content`

The response code depends on the controller implementation:

**Option A ŌĆö `200 OK` with a confirmation message (common Laravel pattern):**

```json
{
  "message": "Category deleted successfully."
}
```

**Option B ŌĆö `204 No Content` (no response body):**

The server returns HTTP `204` with an empty body. This is the REST-standard response for a successful deletion. In Postman, the response body panel will be empty.

> Check your controller's `destroy` method to confirm which response your implementation returns.

#### Error Responses

| Code | Condition | Response Body |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| `404 Not Found` | No category with the given `id` exists in the database | `{"message": "Not Found"}` |
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Database constraint violation, unreachable database, or unhandled exception | `{"message": "Server Error"}` |

**`404` response body:**
```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

This is returned when you attempt to delete a category that does not exist ŌĆö for example, if it was already deleted, or if you used the wrong ID.

**`500` response body (foreign key constraint violation):**
```json
{
  "message": "Server Error"
}
```

In `APP_DEBUG=true` mode, the response will include the full SQL error:
```json
{
  "message": "SQLSTATE[23000]: Integrity constraint violation: 1451 Cannot delete or update a parent row: a foreign key constraint fails",
  "exception": "Illuminate\\Database\\QueryException"
}
```

#### Notes

- Deletion is **permanent and irreversible** by default. To implement recoverable deletion, add Laravel's `SoftDeletes` trait to the `Category` model.
- After a successful deletion, any subsequent `GET /api/categories/{id}` request for the same ID will return `404 Not Found`.
- The deleted category's `id` will **not** be reused by the database auto-increment ŌĆö the next created category will receive a higher ID.
- To implement soft deletes (mark as deleted without removing from the database), add `use SoftDeletes;` to your `Category` model and run a migration to add a `deleted_at` column.

---

## HTTP Status Code Reference

| Code | Name | Used By | Meaning |
|------|------|---------|---------|
| `200` | OK | GET, PATCH, DELETE | Request succeeded. Response body contains the requested data or confirmation. |
| `201` | Created | POST | New resource created successfully. Response body contains the new object with its assigned `id`. |
| `204` | No Content | DELETE | Resource deleted successfully. No response body. |
| `400` | Bad Request | Any | Malformed request syntax. Rarely returned by Laravel directly ŌĆö usually `422` is used instead. |
| `401` | Unauthorized | Any (if auth added) | Authentication credentials are missing or invalid. Returned when Laravel Sanctum/Passport middleware rejects the request. |
| `403` | Forbidden | Any (if auth added) | Authenticated but not authorized to perform this action. |
| `404` | Not Found | Any | The URL does not match any route, or the requested resource ID does not exist. |
| `422` | Unprocessable Entity | POST, PATCH | Request body is syntactically valid JSON but fails Laravel's validation rules. The `errors` object contains field-level messages. |
| `500` | Internal Server Error | Any | Unhandled server-side exception. Check `storage/logs/laravel.log` for details. |

---

## Error Response Structure

All error responses from this API follow a consistent JSON structure when the `Accept: application/json` header is present.

### Validation Error (`422`)

```json
{
  "message": "The name field is required.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "The name field is required."
    ],
    "description": [
      "The description field must be a string."
    ]
  }
}
```

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `message` | `string` | A human-readable summary of the first validation error. |
| `errors` | `object` | A map of field names to arrays of error messages. Each key is a request field that failed validation. |

### General Error (`404`, `500`)

```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `message` | `string` | A brief description of the error. |

In `APP_DEBUG=true` mode, `500` errors also include `exception`, `file`, `line`, and `trace` fields for debugging.

---

## Usage Guidelines

### 1. Start the Laravel Development Server

```bash
php artisan serve
# Starts at http://127.0.0.1:8000 by default
```

### 2. Always Include the Accept Header

Every request must include `Accept: application/json` to ensure all responses ŌĆö including errors ŌĆö are returned as JSON.

### 3. Use a Postman Environment for the Base URL

1. Create a Postman environment called `Laravel Local`.
2. Add variable: `base_url` = `http://127.0.0.1:8000`.
3. Select the environment from the top-right dropdown.
4. Use `{{base_url}}/api/categories` in all request URLs.

### 4. Understand the Status Code Before Reading the Body

| Code | Action |
|------|--------|
| `200` | Read the array (GET) or updated object (PATCH) |
| `201` | Read the created object and save the `id` |
| `204` | No body ŌĆö deletion was successful |
| `404` | Fix the URL or verify the resource ID exists |
| `422` | Read `errors` object and fix the request body |
| `500` | Check `storage/logs/laravel.log` |

### 5. Database Setup

```bash
php artisan migrate        # Create tables
php artisan db:seed        # Optional: seed with sample data
```

### 6. Environment Configuration (`.env`)

```env
APP_ENV=local
APP_DEBUG=true
APP_URL=http://127.0.0.1:8000
DB_CONNECTION=mysql
DB_HOST=127.0.0.1
DB_PORT=3306
DB_DATABASE=laravel
DB_USERNAME=root
DB_PASSWORD=
```

After changing `.env`:
```bash
php artisan config:clear
php artisan cache:clear
```

### 7. Debugging Tips

- **`500` errors:** Run `tail -f storage/logs/laravel.log` to stream live log output.
- **Route not found:** Run `php artisan route:list` to see all registered routes.
- **Stale routes:** Run `php artisan route:clear` after adding new routes.
- **Validation not triggering:** Ensure `Content-Type: application/json` is set on POST/PATCH requests.
- **Empty response body on DELETE:** Your controller may return `204 No Content` ŌĆö this is correct behaviour.

---

## Getting Started Checklist

- [ ] Laravel development server is running: `php artisan serve`
- [ ] Database is running and `.env` credentials are correct
- [ ] Migrations have been applied: `php artisan migrate`
- [ ] Postman environment `Laravel Local` is created with `base_url = http://127.0.0.1:8000`
- [ ] All requests include the `Accept: application/json` header
- [ ] POST and PATCH requests include `Content-Type: application/json` and a valid JSON body
- [ ] You have sent `GET /api/categories` and received a `200 OK` response

request.description:
# Get Categories

Retrieves a complete list of all category resources stored in the database. This is a **read-only** endpoint ŌĆö it does not create, modify, or delete any data. The response is a JSON array where each element represents one category object.

---

## Endpoint

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Method** | `GET` |
| **URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories` |
| **Authentication** | None required |
| **Request Body** | None |
| **Idempotent** | Yes ŌĆö calling this endpoint multiple times always returns the same result without side effects |
| **Safe** | Yes ŌĆö this endpoint never modifies server state |

---

## Required Request Headers

| Header | Value | Required | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `Accept` | `application/json` | **Yes** | Instructs Laravel to return all responses (including errors) as JSON rather than HTML pages |

**How to add this header in Postman:**
1. Open this request.
2. Click the **Headers** tab.
3. Add a new row: Key = `Accept`, Value = `application/json`.
4. Ensure the checkbox on the left is **checked** (enabled).
5. Click **Send**.

> `Content-Type` is **not** required for this request because `GET` requests do not have a request body.

---

## Query Parameters

This endpoint does not accept any query parameters. The full list of categories is always returned. Filtering, sorting, and pagination are not currently implemented.

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| *(none)* | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | No query parameters are supported |

---

## JSON Response Structure

A successful `200 OK` response returns a **JSON array** at the top level. Each element of the array is a **category object**.

### Category Object Fields

| Field | Type | Nullable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `id` | `integer` | No | Unique numeric identifier. Auto-assigned by the database. Immutable. |
| `name` | `string` | No | Human-readable display name of the category. UTF-8 encoded. |
| `description` | `string` | Yes | Optional longer description. Will be `null` if not set. |
| `created_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | UTC timestamp of when the record was created. Set automatically by Laravel. |
| `updated_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | UTC timestamp of the last update. Equals `created_at` if never updated. |

### `id` ŌĆö Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** Positive integer (e.g., `1`, `7`, `42`)
- **Source:** Auto-incremented primary key assigned by the database at creation time
- **Immutable:** Yes ŌĆö never changes after the record is created
- **Unique:** Guaranteed unique across all category records
- **Never zero or negative** in a real database
- **Primary use:** Reference this value when building URLs for single-resource endpoints:
  ```
  GET    /api/categories/1
  PUT    /api/categories/1
  DELETE /api/categories/1
  ```
- **In your front-end:** Store this as the key in dropdowns, lists, and state management

### `name` ŌĆö Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTF-8 string
- **Source:** Provided by the client when the category was created
- **Required at creation:** Yes
- **Mutable:** Yes ŌĆö can be changed via `PUT` or `PATCH`
- **May contain:** Spaces, punctuation, accented characters, Unicode (e.g., `"├ēlectronique"`, `"B├╝cher"`, `"ąÜą░čéąĄą│ąŠčĆč¢čÅ"`)
- **Primary use:** Display to end users in navigation menus, filter dropdowns, product listings

### `description` ŌĆö Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTF-8 string or `null`
- **Source:** Optionally provided by the client at creation or update time
- **Required at creation:** No ŌĆö will be `null` if omitted
- **Mutable:** Yes
- **May contain:** Long-form text, line breaks, Unicode
- **Null handling:** Always check for `null` before rendering:
  ```javascript
  const desc = category.description ?? 'No description provided.';
  ```

### `created_at` ŌĆö Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTC timestamp string, ISO 8601 format
- **Example:** `"2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z"`
- **Source:** Set automatically by Laravel's Eloquent ORM at creation. Cannot be set manually.
- **Immutable:** Yes
- **Parsing in JavaScript:**
  ```javascript
  const date = new Date(category.created_at);
  console.log(date.toLocaleDateString()); // e.g. "1/15/2024"
  ```

### `updated_at` ŌĆö Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTC timestamp string, ISO 8601 format
- **Example:** `"2024-03-22T14:45:00.000000Z"`
- **Source:** Updated automatically by Laravel every time the record is saved
- **Equals `created_at`** when the record has never been modified

### Full Response Example (with data)

```json
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "Electronics",
    "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories",
    "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z"
  },
  {
    "id": 2,
    "name": "Books",
    "description": null,
    "created_at": "2024-01-16T11:00:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-16T11:00:00.000000Z"
  },
  {
    "id": 3,
    "name": "Clothing",
    "description": "Apparel and fashion items",
    "created_at": "2024-01-17T08:45:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-02-01T10:00:00.000000Z"
  }
]
```

### Empty Response Example

```json
[]
```

An empty array is a **valid, successful response**. It means the `categories` table exists but contains no rows. The HTTP status code will still be `200 OK`. Do not treat this as an error.

---

## HTTP Status Codes

### 200 OK

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `200` |
| **Meaning** | Request succeeded. The server queried the database and returned the result. |
| **When you see it** | The Laravel server is running, the database is reachable, and the route is registered |

**Example response:**
```json
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "Electronics",
    "description": "Gadgets and devices",
    "created_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-15T09:30:00.000000Z"
  }
]
```

**What to do:** Read the array and use the data. If the array is empty (`[]`), display an empty state in your UI ŌĆö this is still a success.

---

### 404 Not Found

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `404` |
| **Meaning** | The URL you requested does not match any route registered in Laravel |
| **When you see it** | URL typo, wrong path, or route not registered in `routes/api.php` |

**Common causes for this endpoint:**
- Typing `/api/category` instead of `/api/categories` (missing the `s`)
- Typing `/api/Categories` (wrong case ŌĆö URLs are case-sensitive)
- The route is missing from `routes/api.php`
- The route cache is stale after a code change

**Example response body (with `Accept: application/json`):**
```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Verify the exact URL: `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories`
2. List all registered routes:
   ```bash
   php artisan route:list
   ```
3. Clear the route cache:
   ```bash
   php artisan route:clear
   ```

---

### 500 Internal Server Error

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `500` |
| **Meaning** | The server encountered an unexpected error while processing the request |
| **When you see it** | Database connection failure, PHP exception in the controller, missing table |

**Common causes for this endpoint:**
- The database server is not running
- `DB_HOST`, `DB_PORT`, `DB_DATABASE`, `DB_USERNAME`, or `DB_PASSWORD` in `.env` is incorrect
- The `categories` table does not exist (migrations not run)
- A PHP exception is thrown inside the controller

**Example response body (with `Accept: application/json`, `APP_DEBUG=true`):**
```json
{
  "message": "SQLSTATE[HY000] [2002] Connection refused",
  "exception": "Illuminate\\Database\\QueryException",
  "file": "/var/www/app/vendor/laravel/framework/src/Illuminate/Database/Connection.php",
  "line": 760,
  "trace": []
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Confirm your database server is running.
2. Check your `.env` database credentials.
3. Run pending migrations:
   ```bash
   php artisan migrate
   ```
4. Tail the Laravel log file:
   ```bash
   tail -f storage/logs/laravel.log
   ```
5. Set `APP_DEBUG=true` in `.env` to get a full stack trace in the response.

---

## Usage Guidelines

### Sending the Request from JavaScript (fetch)

```javascript
const response = await fetch('http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories', {
  method: 'GET',
  headers: {
    'Accept': 'application/json'
  }
});

if (!response.ok) {
  throw new Error(`HTTP error: ${response.status}`);
}

const categories = await response.json();
console.log(categories);
// Output: [{ id: 1, name: 'Electronics', description: '...', created_at: '...', updated_at: '...' }, ...]
```

### Using the `id` Field in Subsequent Requests

The `id` returned by this endpoint is the key you use to interact with individual categories:

```
GET    /api/categories/1      ŌåÆ fetch a single category
PUT    /api/categories/1      ŌåÆ update a category
DELETE /api/categories/1      ŌåÆ delete a category
```

Never hardcode IDs ŌĆö always use the values returned by the API, as they differ between development, staging, and production databases.

### Handling a Null `description`

```javascript
categories.forEach(category => {
  const desc = category.description ?? 'No description available.';
  console.log(`${category.name}: ${desc}`);
});
```

### Handling an Empty Array

```javascript
if (categories.length === 0) {
  console.log('No categories yet. Use POST /api/categories to create one.');
} else {
  // render the list
}
```

### Postman Test Scripts

Add these to the **Scripts ŌåÆ Post-response** tab to automatically validate the response:

```javascript
pm.test('Status code is 200', function () {
  pm.response.to.have.status(200);
});

pm.test('Response is a JSON array', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(body).to.be.an('array');
});

pm.test('Each category has id, name, created_at, updated_at', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  body.forEach(function (category) {
    pm.expect(category).to.have.property('id').that.is.a('number');
    pm.expect(category).to.have.property('name').that.is.a('string');
    pm.expect(category).to.have.property('created_at').that.is.a('string');
    pm.expect(category).to.have.property('updated_at').that.is.a('string');
  });
});

pm.test('description is string or null', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  body.forEach(function (category) {
    const desc = category.description;
    pm.expect(desc === null || typeof desc === 'string').to.be.true;
  });
});
```

---

## Prerequisites Checklist

- [ ] Laravel server is running: `php artisan serve`
- [ ] Database server is running and accessible
- [ ] `.env` database credentials are correct
- [ ] Migrations have been applied: `php artisan migrate`
- [ ] `Accept: application/json` header is present and enabled in the Headers tab
- [ ] Correct Postman environment is selected (if using `{{base_url}}`)

response.name: Get Categories

request.description:
# Post Categories

Creates a new category resource in the database. This is a **write** endpoint ŌĆö it inserts a new record and returns the full created object, including the auto-assigned `id` and timestamps. Sending this request multiple times with the same body will create multiple separate category records (it is **not** idempotent).

---

## Endpoint

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Method** | `POST` |
| **URL** | `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories` |
| **Authentication** | None required |
| **Request Body** | JSON object with category fields |
| **Idempotent** | No ŌĆö each call creates a new record |
| **Safe** | No ŌĆö this endpoint modifies server state |

---

## Required Request Headers

| Header | Value | Required | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `Accept` | `application/json` | **Yes** | Instructs Laravel to return all responses (including validation errors) as JSON rather than HTML pages |
| `Content-Type` | `application/json` | **Yes** | Tells Laravel to parse the request body as JSON. Without this, `$request->input('name')` returns `null` and validation fails. |

**How to add these headers in Postman:**
1. Open this request.
2. Click the **Headers** tab.
3. Add two rows:
   - Key = `Accept`, Value = `application/json`
   - Key = `Content-Type`, Value = `application/json`
4. Ensure both checkboxes are **checked** (enabled).

> **Shortcut:** If you select **Body ŌåÆ raw ŌåÆ JSON** in Postman, the `Content-Type: application/json` header is added automatically. You still need to add `Accept: application/json` manually.

---

## Request Body

The request body must be a **JSON object**. Send it in the **Body ŌåÆ raw ŌåÆ JSON** tab in Postman.

### Request Body Fields

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `name` | `string` | **Yes** | The display name of the new category. Must be a non-empty string. |
| `description` | `string` | No | An optional longer description of the category. Omit this field or set it to `null` if not needed. |

### Minimal Request Body (name only)

```json
{
  "name": "Electronics"
}
```

### Full Request Body (name and description)

```json
{
  "name": "Electronics",
  "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories including phones, laptops, and cameras."
}
```

### Field Rules

#### `name`
- **Required:** Yes. Omitting it or sending an empty string will result in a `422 Unprocessable Entity` response.
- **Type:** String. Sending a number or boolean will fail validation.
- **Max length:** Depends on the database column definition (typically 255 characters for a `VARCHAR(255)`).
- **Uniqueness:** Not enforced at the API level unless the database has a unique constraint.
- **Examples of valid values:** `"Books"`, `"Home & Garden"`, `"├ēlectronique"`, `"B├╝cher"`
- **Examples of invalid values:** `""` (empty string), `null`, `123` (number), omitted entirely

#### `description`
- **Required:** No. If omitted, the field will be stored as `null` in the database and returned as `null` in the response.
- **Type:** String or `null`.
- **Max length:** Typically unlimited (stored as `TEXT` in the database).
- **May contain:** Long-form text, line breaks, Unicode characters.

---

## JSON Response Structure

A successful `201 Created` response returns a **JSON object** representing the newly created category.

### Response Fields

| Field | Type | Nullable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `id` | `integer` | No | The unique numeric identifier assigned by the database. Use this in future requests. |
| `name` | `string` | No | The name you provided in the request body. |
| `description` | `string` | Yes | The description you provided, or `null` if not provided. |
| `created_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | UTC timestamp of when the record was created. Set automatically by Laravel. |
| `updated_at` | `string` (ISO 8601) | No | UTC timestamp of the last update. Equals `created_at` immediately after creation. |

### `id` ŌĆö Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** Positive integer (e.g., `1`, `7`, `42`)
- **Source:** Auto-incremented primary key assigned by the database at the moment of insertion
- **Immutable:** Yes ŌĆö never changes after creation
- **Unique:** Guaranteed unique across all category records
- **Critical:** Save this value immediately after a successful `POST`. You will need it to update or delete this category:
  ```
  PUT    /api/categories/{id}
  DELETE /api/categories/{id}
  ```
- **Do not assume sequential IDs** ŌĆö if records have been deleted, there may be gaps (e.g., `1, 2, 5, 6`)

### `name` ŌĆö Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTF-8 string
- **Value:** Exactly what you sent in the request body (after Laravel trims whitespace, if configured)
- **Mutable:** Yes ŌĆö can be changed via `PUT` or `PATCH` endpoints
- **Primary use:** Display to end users in navigation menus, filter dropdowns, product listings

### `description` ŌĆö Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTF-8 string or `null`
- **Value:** What you sent in the request body, or `null` if you omitted the field
- **Mutable:** Yes ŌĆö can be set or updated via `PUT` or `PATCH`
- **Null handling:** Always check for `null` before rendering in your UI:
  ```javascript
  const desc = category.description ?? 'No description provided.';
  ```

### `created_at` ŌĆö Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTC timestamp string, ISO 8601 format
- **Example:** `"2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z"`
- **Source:** Set automatically by Laravel's Eloquent ORM at the moment of insertion. You cannot set this manually.
- **Immutable:** Yes
- **Parsing in JavaScript:**
  ```javascript
  const date = new Date(category.created_at);
  console.log(date.toLocaleDateString()); // e.g. "6/1/2024"
  ```

### `updated_at` ŌĆö Detailed Explanation

- **Type:** UTC timestamp string, ISO 8601 format
- **Value immediately after creation:** Equals `created_at`
- **Source:** Updated automatically by Laravel every time the record is saved

### Full Response Example (with description)

```json
{
  "id": 5,
  "name": "Electronics",
  "description": "Gadgets, devices, and accessories including phones, laptops, and cameras.",
  "created_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z"
}
```

### Full Response Example (without description)

```json
{
  "id": 6,
  "name": "Books",
  "description": null,
  "created_at": "2024-06-01T12:05:00.000000Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-06-01T12:05:00.000000Z"
}
```

---

## HTTP Status Codes

### 201 Created

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `201` |
| **Meaning** | A new category was successfully created and stored in the database |
| **When you see it** | The request body is valid, the server is running, and the database is reachable |

**Example response:**
```json
{
  "id": 5,
  "name": "Sports",
  "description": "Outdoor and fitness equipment",
  "created_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z",
  "updated_at": "2024-06-01T12:00:00.000000Z"
}
```

**What to do:** Store the returned `id` if you need to reference this category in future requests.

---

### 422 Unprocessable Entity

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `422` |
| **Meaning** | The request was well-formed but failed Laravel's validation rules |
| **When you see it** | `name` is missing, empty, or not a string |

**Example: missing `name` field**

Request body sent:
```json
{}
```

Response:
```json
{
  "message": "The name field is required.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "The name field is required."
    ]
  }
}
```

**Example: empty `name` field**

Request body sent:
```json
{
  "name": ""
}
```

Response:
```json
{
  "message": "The name field is required.",
  "errors": {
    "name": [
      "The name field is required."
    ]
  }
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Read the `errors` object ŌĆö each key is a field name, and the value is an array of error messages.
2. Ensure `name` is present and is a non-empty string.
3. Ensure `Content-Type: application/json` is set so Laravel parses the body correctly.
4. Ensure the body is valid JSON (no trailing commas, all strings quoted).

---

### 404 Not Found

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `404` |
| **Meaning** | The URL does not match any route registered in Laravel |
| **When you see it** | URL typo or route not registered in `routes/api.php` |

**Example response body (with `Accept: application/json`):**
```json
{
  "message": "Not Found"
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Verify the URL: `http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories`
2. Check registered routes: `php artisan route:list`
3. Clear route cache: `php artisan route:clear`

---

### 500 Internal Server Error

| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **Code** | `500` |
| **Meaning** | The server encountered an unexpected error while processing the request |
| **When you see it** | Database connection failure, PHP exception, missing table |

**Common causes:**
- The database server is not running
- `.env` database credentials are incorrect
- The `categories` table does not exist (migrations not run)
- A PHP exception is thrown inside the controller

**Example response body (with `APP_DEBUG=true`):**
```json
{
  "message": "SQLSTATE[HY000] [2002] Connection refused",
  "exception": "Illuminate\\Database\\QueryException",
  "file": "/var/www/app/vendor/laravel/framework/src/Illuminate/Database/Connection.php",
  "line": 760,
  "trace": []
}
```

**How to fix:**
1. Confirm your database server is running.
2. Check `.env` database credentials (`DB_HOST`, `DB_PORT`, `DB_DATABASE`, `DB_USERNAME`, `DB_PASSWORD`).
3. Run pending migrations: `php artisan migrate`
4. Tail the log file: `tail -f storage/logs/laravel.log`
5. Set `APP_DEBUG=true` in `.env` to get a full stack trace in the response.

---

## Usage Guidelines

### Sending the Request from JavaScript (fetch)

```javascript
const response = await fetch('http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/categories', {
  method: 'POST',
  headers: {
    'Accept': 'application/json',
    'Content-Type': 'application/json'
  },
  body: JSON.stringify({
    name: 'Electronics',
    description: 'Gadgets, devices, and accessories'
  })
});

if (response.status === 422) {
  const error = await response.json();
  console.error('Validation failed:', error.errors);
} else if (!response.ok) {
  throw new Error(`HTTP error: ${response.status}`);
} else {
  const category = await response.json();
  console.log('Created category with id:', category.id);
}
```

### Saving the Returned `id`

After a successful `201 Created` response, always save the `id` from the response body:

```javascript
const category = await response.json();
const newCategoryId = category.id;
// Use newCategoryId in future GET, PUT, DELETE requests
```

Do not assume what the `id` will be ŌĆö always read it from the response.

### Handling Validation Errors

```javascript
if (response.status === 422) {
  const error = await response.json();
  // error.errors is an object: { fieldName: ["error message", ...] }
  Object.entries(error.errors).forEach(([field, messages]) => {
    console.error(`${field}: ${messages.join(', ')}`);
  });
}
```

### Postman Test Scripts

Add these to the **Scripts ŌåÆ Post-response** tab to automatically validate the response:

```javascript
pm.test('Status code is 201', function () {
  pm.response.to.have.status(201);
});

pm.test('Response is a JSON object', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(body).to.be.an('object').and.not.an('array');
});

pm.test('Response has id (number)', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(body).to.have.property('id').that.is.a('number');
});

pm.test('Response has name (string)', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(body).to.have.property('name').that.is.a('string');
});

pm.test('Response has created_at and updated_at', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(body).to.have.property('created_at').that.is.a('string');
  pm.expect(body).to.have.property('updated_at').that.is.a('string');
});

pm.test('description is string or null', function () {
  const body = pm.response.json();
  const desc = body.description;
  pm.expect(desc === null || typeof desc === 'string').to.be.true;
});

// Save the created id as an environment variable for use in other requests
const body = pm.response.json();
if (body.id) {
  pm.environment.set('category_id', body.id);
  console.log('Saved category_id:', body.id);
}
```

---

## Prerequisites Checklist

- [ ] Laravel server is running: `php artisan serve`
- [ ] Database server is running and accessible
- [ ] `.env` database credentials are correct
- [ ] Migrations have been applied: `php artisan migrate`
- [ ] `Accept: application/json` header is present and enabled
- [ ] `Content-Type: application/json` header is present and enabled
- [ ] Request body is valid JSON with a non-empty `name` field
- [ ] Correct Postman environment is selected (if using `{{base_url}}`)
